result.markdown: --- title: "[trend_test()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-function}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} Test for linear trend across ordered groups using the log-rank test family. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_tests.py#L608-L840){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python trend_test(surv, group, *, scores=None, rho=0.0, gamma=0.0, strata=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} A trend test is a one-degree-of-freedom test for whether survival changes linearly across ordered groups (e.g., disease stages I, II, III, IV or dose levels: low, medium, high). It's more powerful than the multi-degree-of-freedom log-rank test when groups are naturally ordered. The test assigns numeric scores to each group (default: 0, 1, 2, ... for sorted order) and tests whether a linear relationship exists between group score and survival. It can be combined with Fleming-Harrington weights and stratification, just like `logrank_test`. **When to use trend tests**: Use when groups are naturally ordered and you want a higher-power test for a linear trend, rather than testing all possible differences. For exploratory analysis without assuming order, use `logrank_test` instead. ::: ## Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [surv]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A `Surv` response object representing censored survival times. Supports right-censored data or counting-process format. [group]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Group labels (typically ordered categories like 0, 1, 2, 3 for dose levels or stages). Can be a Narwhals series, 1-D array, or Python sequence. Must have at least 2 groups. Must have the same length as `surv`. [scores]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Numeric scores assigned to each group to define the linear trend. If `None` (default), groups are sorted lexicographically and assigned scores 0, 1, 2, ..., (k-1) where k is the number of groups. Provide custom scores as a dictionary mapping group labels to numeric values (e.g., `{1: 0, 2: 1, 3: 2}` for stage labels 1,2,3) to use different scoring (e.g., unequal spacing). Scores can be any real numbers (including negative). [rho]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.0]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Fleming-Harrington weight exponent applied to the pooled survival probability. The default of `0` gives a standard trend test with equal weight across all times. [gamma]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.0]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Fleming-Harrington weight exponent applied to (1 - pooled survival probability). The default of `0`; combined with `rho=1` gives Peto-Peto (Wilcoxon) trend test emphasizing early events. See `logrank_test` for more details on Fleming-Harrington weighting. [strata]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Optional stratifying factor. When provided, the trend test is computed separately within each stratum, then combined (stratified trend test). Use to control for confounding while testing a linear trend. ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [TestResult]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A result object with attributes: - `statistic`: chi-square test statistic (always 1 degree of freedom). - `df`: always `1` for trend tests. - `p_value`: upper-tail chi-square p-value. - `method`: description of the test, e.g., "Linear trend test", "Stratified linear trend test", "G-rho trend test (rho=1, gamma=0)". - `observed`: dictionary mapping each group to observed event count (unweighted). - `expected`: dictionary mapping each group to expected event count under null. ::: ## Details {.doc-details} The test uses a linear contrast with assigned scores: - $U = \sum_i \text{score}[i] \cdot (O[i] - E[i])$ - $V = \sum_i \text{score}[i]^2 \cdot \text{Var}[i]$ - $\chi^2 = U^2 / V \sim \chi^2(1)$ This is equivalent to fitting a Cox model with group encoded as the numeric score and testing whether the coefficient is zero using a score test. ## Examples {.doc-examples} Test for linear trend in survival across ordered cell types: ::: {#92a26061 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Load data and build a right-censored response vet = gw.load_dataset("veteran", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(vet["time"], event=vet["status"]) # Default: cell types are sorted and assigned scores 0,1,2,3 result = gw.trend_test(y, group=vet["celltype"]) result ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` TestResult(method='Linear trend test', statistic=2.3681, df=1, p_value=0.1238) ``` ::: ::: Use custom scores to give different weight to cell type severity: ::: {#9221a1b4 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Scores: adeno and large are low-risk (0,1), smallcell and squamous are high-risk (3,4) scores = {"adeno": 0, "large": 1, "smallcell": 3, "squamous": 4} gw.trend_test(y, group=vet["celltype"], scores=scores) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` TestResult(method='Linear trend test', statistic=1.4479, df=1, p_value=0.2289) ``` ::: ::: Use Peto-Peto weighting to emphasize early differences: ::: {#e4b681a1 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Peto-Peto: rho=1 gives more weight to early event times gw.trend_test(y, group=vet["celltype"], rho=1, gamma=0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` TestResult(method='G-rho trend test (rho=1, gamma=0)', statistic=0.6858, df=1, p_value=0.4076) ``` ::: ::: Use Tarone-Ware weighting to emphasize late differences (gamma=1): ::: {#f6b60a59 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Tarone-Ware: gamma=1 gives more weight to late event times gw.trend_test(y, group=vet["celltype"], rho=0, gamma=1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` TestResult(method='G-rho trend test (rho=0, gamma=1)', statistic=3.7526, df=1, p_value=0.05273) ``` ::: ::: Stratified by treatment to control for treatment effects: ::: {#03ea718f .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Stratify by treatment arm to adjust for treatment effects gw.trend_test(y, group=vet["celltype"], strata=vet["trt"]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` TestResult(method='Stratified linear trend test', statistic=1.7525, df=1, p_value=0.1856) ``` ::: :::
